entry.title: Node.js based HTTP Web/Debug Monitor for SAP BTP
entry.content:
When working on integration and extensibility projects, it’s often handy to be able to “peek into messages.” Recently, I worked with SAP Sales/Service Cloud Version 2 webhooks and wanted to see real messages, transmitted headers, etc., before implementing my webhook logic.

In the past, I’ve used Integration Flows in Integration Suite for this kind of thing, but it felt a bit cumbersome.

Lately, I had the idea of deploying a Node.js app on SAP BTP for this purpose, which provides a debug endpoint for receiving any kind of messages and a small web UI that displays received messages live (as long as you keep the page open in the browser) using SSE (server-sent-events).

WARNING: If the source code shown here is used without modifications (especially without XSUAA), the debug and web UI endpoints are openly accessible on the internet without authentication. Furthermore, the monitor also displays confidential details such as the Authorization header.

 

Source Code

I confess, I've created the main part of the debug monitor with Claude Code 😜 and adapted it further to my needs.
You can find the required files in the following source code snippets. The server.js also includes the stub of a sample webhook that logs the request and after processing also the prepared response to the monitor.

Feel free to use the code and adapt to your needs.

Files

  • public/index.html
  • manifest.yml
  • package.json
  • server.js

 

manifest.yml

---
applications:
  - name: debug-monitor
    memory: 64M
    disk_quota: 512M
    instances: 1
    buildpacks:
      - nodejs_buildpack
    health-check-type: http
    health-check-http-endpoint: /health

 

package.json

{
  "type": "module",
  "name": "debug-monitor",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "HTTP Debug Monitor",
  "main": "server.js",
  "scripts": {
    "start": "node server.js"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "express": "^4.21.2"
  },
  "engines": {
    "node": "24"
  }
}

 

server.js

import express from 'express'
import { fileURLToPath } from 'url'
import path from 'path'

const PORT = process.env.PORT || 3000

const __dirname = path.dirname(fileURLToPath(import.meta.url))

const app = express()
app.use(express.json({ limit: '5mb' }))
app.use('/', express.static(path.join(__dirname, 'public')))

// SSE Clients
const sseClients = new Set()

/**
 * Send a record to the debug monitor
 */
function pushEntryToDebugMonitor(entry)
{
  if (sseClients.size === 0)
      return;

  const payload = JSON.stringify({
     id: crypto.randomUUID(),
     timestamp: new Date().toISOString(),
     ...entry 
  });

  sseClients.forEach(client => client.write(`data: ${payload}\n\n`))
}

/**
 * Prepare a debug record and send it to the monitor
 * @Param {*} source - caption in the monitor
 * @Param {*} req - request object
 * @Param {*} resBody - response body (if available)
 * @Param {*} isFollowUp - just a flag for the monitor for displaying -> e.g. when sending the request body before processing and the request+response after processing to the debug monitor
 */
function pushToDebugMonitor(source, req, resBody = null, isFollowUp = false)
{
  const entry = {
    source: source,
    method: req.method,
    url: req.originalUrl,
    httpVersion: req.httpVersion,
    query: req.query,
    headers: req.headers,
    requestBody: req.body,
    responseBody: resBody,
    isFollowUp: isFollowUp
  };

  pushEntryToDebugMonitor(entry);
}

/**
 * General debug endpoint to send data to the debug monitor
 */
app.all(['/debug', '/debug/*'], (req, res) => {
  pushToDebugMonitor('Debug Endpoint', req);
  res.status(200).json({ ok: true })
})

/**
 * Debug monitor: SSE stream for the UI
 */
app.get('/debug-events', (req, res) => {
  res.setHeader('Content-Type', 'text/event-stream');
  res.setHeader('Cache-Control', 'no-cache');
  res.setHeader('Connection', 'keep-alive');
  res.flushHeaders();
  sseClients.add(res);
  req.on('close', () => sseClients.delete(res));
})

/**
 * Health check endpoint
 */
app.get('/health', (req, res) => {
  res.json({
    status: 'ok',
    service: 'HTTP Debug Monitor',
    timestamp: new Date().toISOString()
  })
})

/**
 * Sample Webhook
 */
app.post('/webhooks/visit/posthook', async (req, res) => {

  try
  {
    // add the request to the debug monitor
    pushToDebugMonitor('Visit Posthook Req.', req);

    // process the request ...

    // add the request + response to the debug monitor (responseBody was created during processing)
    pushToDebugMonitor('Visit Posthook Resp.', req, responseBody, true);
  }
  catch (error)
  {
    console.error('❌ Error in webhook-visit-post:', error)
    res.status(500).json({ error: 'Internal server error' })
  }
});

app.listen(PORT, () => {
  console.log(`🛜 Listening on port ${PORT}`);
});

 

index.html

Don't forget that the server.js delivers static files from the public folder. Hence, the index.html must be placed in the subfolder "public".

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
  <title>Debug Monitor</title>
  <style>
    * { box-sizing: border-box; margin: 0; padding: 0; }

    body {
      font-family: -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, 'Segoe UI', sans-serif;
      font-size: 13px;
      background: #ffffff;
      color: #212529;
      height: 100vh;
      display: flex;
      flex-direction: column;
      overflow: hidden;
    }

    header {
      display: flex;
      align-items: center;
      gap: 10px;
      padding: 10px 16px;
      background: #f0f0f0;
      border-bottom: 1px solid #dee2e6;
      flex-shrink: 0;
    }

    header h1 { font-size: 14px; font-weight: 600; letter-spacing: 0.05em; color: #6f42c1; }

    .live-indicator {
      display: flex;
      align-items: center;
      gap: 5px;
      font-size: 11px;
      color: #6c757d;
    }

    .live-dot {
      width: 8px;
      height: 8px;
      border-radius: 50%;
      background: #ced4da;
    }
    .live-dot.connected    { background: #16a34a; box-shadow: 0 0 6px #16a34a; }
    .live-dot.disconnected { background: #dc2626; }

    .spacer { flex: 1; }

    .btn {
      padding: 4px 10px;
      border-radius: 4px;
      border: 1px solid #ced4da;
      background: #ffffff;
      color: #212529;
      font-size: 12px;
      cursor: pointer;
    }
    .btn:hover { background: #e9ecef; }

    .layout {
      display: flex;
      flex: 1;
      overflow: hidden;
    }

    /* Sidebar */
    .sidebar {
      width: 280px;
      flex-shrink: 0;
      border-right: 1px solid #dee2e6;
      display: flex;
      flex-direction: column;
      overflow: hidden;
    }

    .sidebar-header {
      padding: 8px 12px;
      font-size: 11px;
      color: #6c757d;
      text-transform: uppercase;
      letter-spacing: 0.08em;
      border-bottom: 1px solid #dee2e6;
      flex-shrink: 0;
    }

    .message-list { flex: 1; overflow-y: auto; }

    .message-item {
      padding: 8px 12px;
      cursor: pointer;
      border-bottom: 1px solid #f0f0f0;
      border-left: 2px solid transparent;
      display: flex;
      flex-direction: column;
      gap: 3px;
    }
    .message-item:hover  { background: #f8f9fa; }
    .message-item.active { background: #e9ecef; border-left-color: #6f42c1; }

    .item-row { display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 6px; }
    .item-time   { font-size: 10px; color: #6c757d; font-family: monospace; flex-shrink: 0; }
    .item-url    { font-size: 11px; color: #343a40; overflow: hidden; text-overflow: ellipsis; white-space: nowrap; flex: 1; min-width: 0; }
    .follow-up-icon { font-size: 11px; flex-shrink: 0; opacity: 0.6; }
    .item-source { font-size: 10px; color: #6c757d; font-style: italic; overflow: hidden; text-overflow: ellipsis; white-space: nowrap; }

    .method-badge {
      font-size: 10px;
      font-weight: 700;
      padding: 1px 5px;
      border-radius: 3px;
      flex-shrink: 0;
      font-family: monospace;
    }
    .method-GET    { background: #d1fae5; color: #065f46; }
    .method-POST   { background: #dbeafe; color: #1e40af; }
    .method-PUT    { background: #fef3c7; color: #92400e; }
    .method-PATCH  { background: #d1fae5; color: #065f46; }
    .method-DELETE { background: #fee2e2; color: #991b1b; }
    .method-OTHER  { background: #f3f4f6; color: #374151; }

    /* Detail view */
    .detail {
      flex: 1;
      display: flex;
      flex-direction: column;
      overflow: hidden;
    }

    .empty-state {
      flex: 1;
      display: flex;
      align-items: center;
      justify-content: center;
      color: #adb5bd;
      font-size: 13px;
    }

    .detail-content {
      flex: 1;
      display: flex;
      flex-direction: column;
      overflow: hidden;
      padding: 16px;
      gap: 14px;
    }

    /* Request line */
    .request-line {
      font-family: 'Cascadia Code', 'JetBrains Mono', 'Fira Code', monospace;
      font-size: 14px;
      display: flex;
      align-items: baseline;
      gap: 10px;
      flex-wrap: wrap;
      flex-shrink: 0;
    }
    .request-method  { font-weight: 700; font-size: 15px; }
    .request-url     { color: #0077aa; word-break: break-all; }
    .request-version { color: #6c757d; font-size: 12px; }

    /* Sections */
    .section { display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 6px; flex-shrink: 0; }
    .section.payload-section { flex: 1; overflow: hidden; display: flex; flex-direction: column; min-height: 120px; }

    .section-title {
      font-size: 11px;
      font-weight: 600;
      text-transform: uppercase;
      letter-spacing: 0.07em;
      color: #6c757d;
    }

    /* Tables */
    .table-wrap { overflow-x: auto; }
    table { width: 100%; border-collapse: collapse; font-size: 12px; }
    th {
      text-align: left;
      padding: 5px 8px;
      background: #f0f0f0;
      color: #6c757d;
      font-weight: 600;
      font-size: 11px;
      border-bottom: 1px solid #dee2e6;
    }
    td {
      padding: 4px 8px;
      border-bottom: 1px solid #f0f0f0;
      vertical-align: top;
      word-break: break-word;
    }
    td:first-child { color: #1e40af; font-family: monospace; white-space: nowrap; width: 220px; }
    tr:hover td { background: #f8f9fa; }

    /* Payload textarea */
    .payload-area {
      flex: 1;
      min-height: 0;
      width: 100%;
      background: #f8f9fa;
      color: #1a5c38;
      font-family: 'Cascadia Code', 'JetBrains Mono', 'Fira Code', monospace;
      font-size: 12px;
      border: 1px solid #dee2e6;
      border-radius: 4px;
      padding: 10px;
      resize: none;
      outline: none;
    }

    /* Collapsible section title */
    .collapsible { cursor: pointer; user-select: none; display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 4px; }
    .collapsible:hover { color: #495057; }
    .toggle-arrow { font-size: 8px; }

    /* Payload tab bar */
    .payload-header { display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 10px; }
    .tab-bar { display: flex; gap: 1px; }
    .tab {
      padding: 2px 12px;
      font-size: 11px;
      font-weight: 500;
      border: 1px solid #dee2e6;
      border-radius: 3px 3px 0 0;
      background: #f0f0f0;
      color: #6c757d;
      cursor: pointer;
      font-family: inherit;
    }
    .tab:hover { background: #e9ecef; color: #495057; }
    .tab.tab-active { background: #ffffff; color: #212529; font-weight: 600; border-bottom-color: #ffffff; }

    ::-webkit-scrollbar { width: 6px; height: 6px; }
    ::-webkit-scrollbar-track { background: transparent; }
    ::-webkit-scrollbar-thumb { background: #ced4da; border-radius: 3px; }
  </style>
</head>
<body>

<header>
  <h1>DEBUG MONITOR</h1>
  <div class="live-indicator">
    <div class="live-dot" id="liveDot"></div>
    <span id="liveLabel">Connecting…</span>
  </div>
  <div class="spacer"></div>
  <button class="btn" onclick="clearMessages()">Clear</button>
</header>

<div class="layout">
  <aside class="sidebar">
    <div class="sidebar-header">Messages (<span id="msgCount">0</span>)</div>
    <div class="message-list" id="messageList"></div>
  </aside>
  <main class="detail" id="detail">
    <div class="empty-state" id="emptyState">Waiting for messages…</div>
    <div class="detail-content" id="detailContent" style="display:none"></div>
  </main>
</div>

<script>
  const messages = []
  let selectedId = null
  let headersCollapsed = false
  let activePayloadTab = 'request'

  function connect() {
    const es = new EventSource('/debug-events')
    es.onopen  = () => setLive(true)
    es.onerror = () => setLive(false)
    es.onmessage = e => {
      const msg = JSON.parse(e.data)
      messages.unshift(msg)
      renderList()
      if (messages.length === 1 || selectedId === null) selectMessage(msg.id)
    }
  }

  function setLive(ok) {
    document.getElementById('liveDot').className = 'live-dot ' + (ok ? 'connected' : 'disconnected')
    document.getElementById('liveLabel').textContent = ok ? 'Live' : 'Disconnected'
  }

  function methodClass(method) {
    const m = (method || '').toUpperCase()
    return ['GET', 'POST', 'PUT', 'PATCH', 'DELETE'].includes(m) ? `method-${m}` : 'method-OTHER'
  }

  function formatTime(ts) {
    return new Date(ts).toLocaleTimeString([], { hour: '2-digit', minute: '2-digit', second: '2-digit' })
  }

  function esc(str) {
    return String(str)
      .replace(/&/g, '&')
      .replace(/</g, '<')
      .replace(/>/g, '>')
      .replace(/"/g, '"')
  }

  function renderList() {
    document.getElementById('msgCount').textContent = messages.length
    document.getElementById('messageList').innerHTML = messages.map(m => `
      <div class="message-item ${m.id === selectedId ? 'active' : ''}" onclick="selectMessage('${m.id}')">
        <div class="item-row">
          <span class="method-badge ${methodClass(m.method)}">${esc(m.method || '?')}</span>
          <span class="item-url">${esc(m.url || '')}</span>
          ${m.isFollowUp ? '<span class="follow-up-icon">🔗</span>' : ''}
        </div>
        <div class="item-row">
          <span class="item-time">${formatTime(m.timestamp)}</span>
          <span class="item-source">${esc(m.source || '')}</span>
        </div>
      </div>
    `).join('')
  }

  function selectMessage(id) {
    selectedId = id
    renderList()

    const msg = messages.find(m => m.id === id)
    if (!msg) return

    document.getElementById('emptyState').style.display = 'none'
    const content = document.getElementById('detailContent')
    content.style.display = 'flex'

    let html = `
      <div class="request-line">
        <span class="request-method ${methodClass(msg.method)}">${esc(msg.method || '')}</span>
        <span class="request-url">${esc(msg.url || '')}</span>
        <span class="request-version">HTTP/${esc(msg.httpVersion || '')}</span>
      </div>
    `

    const queryEntries = Object.entries(msg.query || {})
    if (queryEntries.length > 0) {
      html += `
        <div class="section">
          <div class="section-title">Query Parameters</div>
          <div class="table-wrap"><table>
            <thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Value</th></tr></thead>
            <tbody>${queryEntries.map(([k, v]) => `<tr><td>${esc(k)}</td><td>${esc(String(v))}</td></tr>`).join('')}</tbody>
          </table></div>
        </div>
      `
    }

    html += `
      <div class="section">
        <div class="section-title collapsible" onclick="toggleHeaders()">
          <span id="headersArrow" class="toggle-arrow">${headersCollapsed ? '▶' : '▼'}</span> Headers
        </div>
        <div class="table-wrap" id="headersTableWrap" style="display:${headersCollapsed ? 'none' : ''}">
          <table>
            <thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Value</th></tr></thead>
            <tbody>${Object.entries(msg.headers || {}).map(([k, v]) => `<tr><td>${esc(k)}</td><td>${esc(String(v))}</td></tr>`).join('')}</tbody>
          </table>
        </div>
      </div>
    `

    const hasResponse = msg.responseBody != null && msg.responseBody !== ''
    const hasTabs = msg.requestBody !== undefined || hasResponse

    if (hasTabs && !hasResponse && activePayloadTab === 'response') activePayloadTab = 'request'

    if (hasTabs) {
      html += `
        <div class="section payload-section">
          <div class="payload-header">
            <div class="tab-bar">
              <button id="tabBtnRequest" class="tab ${activePayloadTab === 'request' ? 'tab-active' : ''}" onclick="setPayloadTab('request')">Request</button>
              ${hasResponse ? `<button id="tabBtnResponse" class="tab ${activePayloadTab === 'response' ? 'tab-active' : ''}" onclick="setPayloadTab('response')">Response</button>` : ''}
            </div>
            <span class="section-title">Body / Payload</span>
          </div>
          <textarea id="payloadRequest" class="payload-area" readonly style="display:${activePayloadTab === 'request' ? '' : 'none'}">${esc(prettyJson(msg.requestBody))}</textarea>
          ${hasResponse ? `<textarea id="payloadResponse" class="payload-area" readonly style="display:${activePayloadTab === 'response' ? '' : 'none'}">${esc(prettyJson(msg.responseBody))}</textarea>` : ''}
        </div>
      `
    } else {
      html += `
        <div class="section payload-section">
          <div class="section-title">Body / Payload</div>
          <textarea class="payload-area" readonly>${esc(prettyJson(msg.requestBody))}</textarea>
        </div>
      `
    }

    content.innerHTML = html
  }

  function prettyJson(val) {
    if (val === undefined || val === null) return '(empty)'
    if (typeof val === 'string') {
      try { return JSON.stringify(JSON.parse(val), null, 2) } catch { return val }
    }
    try { return JSON.stringify(val, null, 2) } catch { return String(val) }
  }

  function clearMessages() {
    messages.length = 0
    selectedId = null
    document.getElementById('msgCount').textContent = '0'
    document.getElementById('messageList').innerHTML = ''
    document.getElementById('emptyState').style.display = 'flex'
    document.getElementById('detailContent').style.display = 'none'
  }

  function toggleHeaders() {
    headersCollapsed = !headersCollapsed
    const wrap = document.getElementById('headersTableWrap')
    const arrow = document.getElementById('headersArrow')
    if (wrap)  wrap.style.display = headersCollapsed ? 'none' : ''
    if (arrow) arrow.textContent   = headersCollapsed ? '▶' : '▼'
  }

  function setPayloadTab(tab) {
    activePayloadTab = tab
    const reqEl = document.getElementById('payloadRequest')
    const resEl = document.getElementById('payloadResponse')
    const tabReq = document.getElementById('tabBtnRequest')
    const tabRes = document.getElementById('tabBtnResponse')
    if (reqEl)  reqEl.style.display = tab === 'request' ? '' : 'none'
    if (resEl)  resEl.style.display = tab === 'response' ? '' : 'none'
    if (tabReq) tabReq.classList.toggle('tab-active', tab === 'request')
    if (tabRes) tabRes.classList.toggle('tab-active', tab === 'response')
  }

  connect()
</script>
</body>
</html>

 

Useful Commands and Deployment

# Install Dependencies:
npm install

# Run Locally (to test):
npm start

# Login and Deploy to BTP:
cf login --sso
cf push

Happy Integrating and Extending! 🥳

entry.title: Post-SAP Sapphire briefing SAP Business AI Platform – GOVERN
entry.content:
Every week, there’s a new AI tool, a new agent, a new model.  Honestly, it can feel a bit like watching new apps appear on a teenager’s phone overnight.

Now imagine that happening inside an enterprise landscape.

That’s why we’re hosting the webinar “Post-SAP Sapphire briefing SAP Business AI Platform – GOVERN”.  We’ll dive into how SAP enables organizations to centrally discover, verify, and govern AI agents, LLMs, and MCP servers across different environments.  One place. Better visibility. Clearer accountability.

For partners, this opens an important conversation with customers.  AI adoption is accelerating, but governance, trust, and compliance still matter deeply.  In many cases, they matter more than the model itself.

If you joined SAP Sapphire conversations around Business AI, this session helps connect the dots with practical governance capabilities and real operational value.

Plus, bring your questions. We’ll end with a live Q&A session.

Register now.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/may/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00014253.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #SAPBusinessAI #EnterpriseAI #DigitalTransformation #AICompliance

entry.title: Joule Studio at SAP Sapphire 2026: Ten Days, Two Cities, One Clear Signal
entry.content:
As a Product Manager for SAP Build, focused on Early Adopter and Product Enablement, SAP Sapphire is one of the most important moments in the year - not just to present what we have built, but to hear directly from the people building with it. This year, the SAP Build PM team was deeply embedded across both events: in the ASUG pre-conference, the keynotes, the sessions, and the Agent Lab. We asked our PM colleagues to do what product managers do best - observe closely, listen carefully, and capture what they saw. This post is the result of that: a collective picture of what Joule Studio's debut at Sapphire looked and felt like, from the people who were there.

SAP Sapphire 2026 was held across two cities - Orlando (May 11–13) and Madrid (May 19–21) - and the energy in both was unmistakable. Joule Studio made its public debut at Sapphire. Thousands of customers, partners, and developers experienced it hands-on: some for the first time, some building on what they had already seen in Florida. The conversation that started in the US continued in Europe with the same momentum and the same conclusion. Enterprise AI has stopped being a promise and started being something you can build in an afternoon.

 

Orlando: Learning by Building

SAP Sapphire Orlando set the tone for what this year's event would be about. Christian Klein opened the keynote with a question that reframes everything: will SAP even be a software company in the future? His answer - that SAP is becoming a business AI company - was not defensive. It was clarifying.

The core argument landed clearly: AI keeps stalling in enterprises not because the models are lacking, but because they are trained on the public internet, not on your business. They have no knowledge of your processes, your data, or your compliance framework. SAP Business AI Platform - and Joule Studio within it - is built around fixing exactly that.

Christian Klein introducing SAP Business AI Platform during the Keynote

Joule Studio was introduced as an intent-based, deeply contextualized build environment for agents, open to any third-party model with no lock-in and both low-code and pro-code paths - with agent development free and backed by a €100M+ ecosystem investment. Throughout the event, there was a noticeable shift away from isolated AI use cases toward AI-infused end-to-end experiences. Attendees were not looking for another AI experiment - they wanted proof that this works in production. Real-world customer journeys, with Fujitsu cited as a concrete reference multiple times, drove that conversation.

Muhammad Alam introducing SAP Autonomous Suite during the Keynote

The keynote by Dr. Michael Ameling, Jonathan von Rüden, Jan Oberhauser, André Christ, and Maria Kondratyeva featured a live Joule Studio demonstration that became the defining moment of the event. The team demonstrated a 3-way invoice matching agent built end-to-end on stage. The scenario was immediately relatable: a company processing 50,000 invoices monthly, chronic matching exceptions, and approvals consistently landing with the wrong people.

Joule Studio received a natural language description of the problem. What followed was automated and complete: SAP LeanIX integration discovering the relevant SAP systems, SAP Signavio applying 50 years of SAP process expertise to understand matching strategies and routing rules, generated orchestration workflows for exception handling and escalation, and Agent Hub providing governance from the first deployment.

The result: Accounts Payable (AP) exception cycle time reduced from 72 hours to 90 seconds. Five manual touchpoints eliminated to zero.

One audience reaction said it all: “I couldn't tell if it was a real system or a guided demo.”  That comment - part skepticism, part disbelief - repeated throughout the week. The bar for proving production-readiness is high. Joule Studio cleared it.

Christian Klein introducing the Autonomous Enterprise during the Keynote

 

The Agent Lab: From Skepticism to Conviction

If the keynote introduced Joule Studio, the Agent Lab made it personal. Over three days, more than 1,000 unique customers and partners queued up and nobody left early. The numbers are striking: 3,366 AI solutions created, 1,640 agents built, across Finance, HR, Supply Chain, and beyond.

The Agent Lab in Orlando

The experience followed a consistent pattern: skepticism at the start, hands-on building in the middle, and genuine surprise at the end. The barrier was never ideas - it was trust that the platform would actually work. Once they saw their own business problem translated into a working design, that trust arrived instantly.

As Samir Hamichi from the Agent Lab team observed: “People were looking to know the added value from SAP, what [was] the differentiator. When they walked through the experience, they understood the power of tools and strategy.”

The Agent Lab in Orlando

 

What Makes Joule Studio Different

Every AI coding tool on the market can write code. What they cannot do is understand your business. Generic tools operate at the file and repository level - they have no knowledge of your procure-to-pay process, your enterprise architecture, or the SAP capabilities already available to you. Context is lost at every handoff.

Joule Studio changes the starting point entirely. The SAP Knowledge Graph, process models from SAP Signavio, enterprise architecture from SAP LeanIX, and SAP Domain Models all flow automatically into every development artifact from the first session. A business analyst describes a problem in plain language. Joule Studio generates a structured, SAP-context-aware Product Requirements Document - a shared source of truth for business stakeholders, developers, and architects, aligned from the first output rather than after weeks of back-and-forth. That PRD becomes a Technical Specification; the specification becomes production-grade, clean-core-aligned code, deployable to a fully managed runtime.

The insight heard repeatedly across the event from customers: “This brings business back into the loop.” And from one partner architect: “Context at scale - Signavio, LeanIX. That is powerful.”

One participant shared on social media what struck with him most: the end-to-end completeness. From describing the intent to generating a PRD, functional and technical specifications, unit tests, and a deployable solution - all without leaving the platform. What surprised him most was that the platform itself guides you through the process, asking clarifying questions like a colleague would rather than expecting you to have all the answers upfront. As he put it, you are not just building agents - you are building them with the help of agents.

You are not building an agent that connects to your business afterward. You are building one that is grounded in it from the first sentence you type.

SAP Business AI Platform

 

The Governance Conversation Nobody Skips

One exchange at the Orlando Agent Lab deserves to be named directly. An attendee, after seeing what agents could do, said: “Now I can cut half my company.”

Maria Kondratyeva pushed back immediately: “That is not what agents are for. Agents eliminate soul-crushing manual work - data entry, chasing approvals, reconciling exceptions - so people can focus on work that requires judgment, creativity, and relationship-building. The work only humans can do.”

But the fact that the question was asked reflects how seriously people are taking this. The governance features in Joule Studio - Agent Hub, LeanIX integration, and human-in-the-loop approval gates - are not compliance checkboxes. They are what separates enterprise AI from unmanageable risk. The governance story needs to be as prominent as the capability story - and across both cities, the sessions that included it landed better every time.

 

Madrid: The Momentum Crosses the Atlantic

One week later, Madrid picked up exactly where Orlando left off. Dr. Michael Ameling, Jonathan von Rüden, Jan Oberhauser, André Christ, and Soufia Maherzi brought the same invoice matching demonstration to a European audience and got the same reaction. A fresh crowd, the same disbelief turning into belief.

The Madrid Agent Lab wrapped up with 765 customers and partners, 1,350 AI solutions created, and 770 agents built. Different city, same energy: long queues, nobody leaving, and that consistent moment of surprise when an attendee's business problem became a fully designed agent before their eyes.

As Friederike Marby recalled: “Agent Lab was intense in the best possible way. The sheer volume of feedback, the questions, the ideas flying around. We had customers who flat-out didn't believe it was real, almost looking for reasons to dismiss it. But what really stayed with me was watching them discover, on their own terms, that it absolutely was.”

 The Madrid Agent Lab Team

 

Final Thoughts

What struck us most, putting this post together, was the consistency of what our colleagues reported across both cities - independent observations that told the same story. The skepticism at the keynote. The energy in the Agent Lab. The moment attendees stopped asking “can this really work?” and started asking “what do I build first?”

That shift - from doubt to conviction, not through a polished pitch but through direct, hands-on experience - is exactly what we set out to enable. Joule Studio's debut at Sapphire was not just a product launch. It was the beginning of a much larger conversation between SAP and the enterprise community about what it means to build with AI in a way that is grounded, governed, and genuinely useful.

We are grateful to our PM colleagues whose impressions and observations brought this post to life - and to every customer, partner, and developer who showed up, asked hard questions, and built something.

 

What is Next

Across two cities and six days of hands-on building, the combined numbers tell their own story: thousands of agents created, thousands of attendees who arrived skeptical and left with a deployment plan.

Joule Studio is planned to reach General Availability in Q3 2026. The trial is free, and the starting point is a business problem you already know you have.

 

Want to watch the sessions?

- Listen to the keynote - The Beginning of Better

- Discover the Demo - The Business AI Platform for your future

- Watch the Deep Dive - From intent to execution: Build AI agents with Joule Studio

- Hear the Strategy - The enterprise path to agentic AI

All Sapphire recordings are available here

Register for upcoming webinars here

 

Ready to start building?

Visit Joule Studio to learn more and register your interest.

Explore the Autonomous Enterprise Learning Journey for guided tutorials and deeper dives into specific capabilities.

The enterprise AI era is not approaching. In Orlando and Madrid, SAP customers and partners are already building it, one agent at a time.

entry.title: Positioning SAP Business Data Cloud for the Retail industry
entry.content:
Retail leaders are being asked to do more with less. More personalization. More resilience. More visibility. Yet only 34% of business leaders fully trust their data.

That gap creates a huge opportunity for SAP Partners.

In our upcoming webinar, Positioning SAP Business Data Cloud for the Retail Industry, we'll go beyond product messaging and explore how SAP Business Data Cloud fits into a modern retail architecture.

We'll cover how SAP BDC brings together SAP and non-SAP data, supports predictive replenishment and demand forecasting, enables the data product economy, and lays the foundation for autonomous retail powered by Agentic AI.

Plus, you'll see a live demo and learn how to connect these capabilities to executive priorities across merchandising, pricing, inventory, and supply chain operations.

Whether you implement solutions, build extensions, or lead customer conversations, this session will help you position SAP BDC with greater confidence and credibility.

Register now and join us.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/may/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00014218.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #SAPBusinessDataCloud #RetailIndustry #BusinessAI #RetailTransformation

entry.title: What 20+ Years of Working with SAP Has Taught Me About Successful Transformations
entry.content:
What 20+ Years of Working with SAP Has Taught Me About Successful Transformations

Introduction

Over the past two decades, I have had the opportunity to work on SAP programs across multiple industries, including manufacturing, distribution, consumer products, and food services. During this journey, I have participated in SAP implementations, upgrades, global rollouts, business process transformations, and large-scale SAP S/4HANA migration programs.

Technology has changed significantly during this time. We have moved from traditional ERP implementations to intelligent enterprise platforms powered by real-time analytics, cloud technologies, automation, and artificial intelligence. However, despite these advancements, I have noticed that the factors that determine transformation success remain surprisingly consistent.

This article shares some of the most important lessons I have learned from working on SAP programs over the years.

Technology Alone Does Not Guarantee Success

Early in my career, I believed that selecting the right technology would automatically lead to project success. Over time, I learned that technology is only one part of the equation.

I have seen technically sound implementations struggle because business users were not prepared, process changes were not fully understood, or organizational alignment was missing.

The most successful SAP programs focus equally on people, processes, governance, and technology.

Organizations that treat SAP transformations as business transformations rather than IT projects typically achieve better outcomes.

Data Quality Matters More Than Most Organizations Expect

One recurring challenge I have observed across SAP programs is data quality.

Many organizations underestimate the effort required to cleanse, standardize, and validate data before migration activities begin.

In several transformation programs, data issues became one of the largest sources of testing delays, reconciliation challenges, and post-go-live support incidents.

My experience has taught me that data preparation should begin early and remain a continuous focus throughout the transformation lifecycle.

The quality of the migrated data often determines how quickly organizations realize value after deployment.

Governance Should Be Proactive, Not Reactive

Another lesson I have learned is that governance cannot be treated as a reporting exercise.

Many projects rely heavily on status updates and issue tracking. While these activities are important, effective governance requires early identification of risks and proactive decision-making.

The strongest transformation programs establish clear escalation paths, executive involvement, readiness monitoring, and structured decision-making processes from the beginning.

When governance becomes reactive, issues often grow larger before corrective actions are taken.

Business Engagement Is Critical

SAP transformations affect how people perform their daily work.

Because of this, business participation cannot be limited to requirements gathering and testing activities.

Successful programs consistently involve business stakeholders throughout the project lifecycle.

This includes:

  • Process design reviews

  • Data validation activities

  • Testing participation

  • Readiness assessments

  • Change management initiatives

The more engaged the business becomes, the smoother the transition tends to be after go-live.

Testing Is About Confidence, Not Just Defect Detection

Many organizations view testing primarily as a mechanism for identifying defects.

While defect identification is important, testing serves a broader purpose.

Testing builds confidence.

It helps project teams, business users, and leadership understand whether the organization is truly prepared for deployment.

When testing is approached as a business validation exercise rather than a technical checkpoint, the quality of outcomes often improves significantly.

The Importance of Executive Sponsorship

Throughout my career, I have observed a direct relationship between executive engagement and project success.

Executive sponsors help:

  • Remove organizational barriers

  • Resolve cross-functional conflicts

  • Secure resources

  • Accelerate decision-making

  • Reinforce transformation objectives

Programs with strong executive sponsorship typically maintain better momentum and experience fewer delays.

Go-Live Is Not the Finish Line

One of the biggest misconceptions in SAP programs is that success is measured by reaching go-live.

In reality, go-live represents the beginning of a new operational phase.

The true measure of success includes:

  • User adoption

  • Business process stability

  • Operational performance

  • Issue resolution effectiveness

  • Achievement of business objectives

Organizations that invest in hypercare, support readiness, and continuous improvement often realize greater long-term value from their SAP investments.

The Growing Role of AI in SAP Transformations

Recent advancements in artificial intelligence are creating new opportunities for SAP programs.

AI is increasingly being used to support:

  • Data quality monitoring

  • Predictive analytics

  • Risk identification

  • Testing optimization

  • Project governance

  • Operational insights

While AI is not a replacement for experience and judgment, it can significantly improve visibility and decision-making when applied appropriately.

I believe AI will continue to play an increasingly important role in future SAP transformation programs.

Final Thoughts

After more than 20 years of working with SAP, one lesson stands out above all others.

Successful transformations are rarely driven by technology alone.

They succeed when organizations align people, processes, data, governance, leadership, and technology toward a common objective.

Every SAP transformation presents unique challenges, but the principles of preparation, collaboration, business engagement, and continuous improvement remain constant.

As SAP technologies continue to evolve, these fundamentals will remain just as important as the platforms themselves.

From my experience, organizations that focus on these fundamentals consistently position themselves for stronger transformation outcomes and long-term business success.

entry.title: What We Learned Hosting a SAP Document AI Workshop
entry.content:
Introduction

Some weeks ago we had a hands-on event focused on SAP Document AI and enterprise document automation. We had planned it to be just a technical workshop, yet the attendees wasted no time in telling us all kinds of complications they were experiencing at the time. Soon, we were talking about operational processes, workflow complexity, and the increasing importance of AI in enterprise environments.

When we were planning the workshop we did not just want to show people a product. Our goal was to create a space where participants could engage directly with actual document processing situations, as well as investigate automation options that might enhance their routine workflows. I mean, you can't have an opinion about something you haven't used or experienced yourself.

During the session attendees had the opportunity to experiment with document classification, extraction models, workflow routing and automation processes related to larger SAP workflows.

As the Hackathon went on, the conversations naturally shifted toward some of the operational challenges many companies are currently facing. Although SAP Document AI was the focus, there was also room for repetitive manual work, fragmented validation processes, inconsistent document formats, and the difficulty of scaling document-heavy operations across teams and business units.

 

What the workshop was about

The workshop included product exploration and practical activities, simulating real enterprise scenarios. Rather than merely attend presentations or demos, participants were able to directly engage with workflows, classifiers, extraction schemas, and automation processes.

The event provided participants with insights into how SAP Document AI can support invoicing processes, free-form documents, workflow routing, and document classification.

 

One exercise that participants seemed to find particularly useful was the ROI calculator we brought to the workshop. While most of the day focused on workflows and automation scenarios, the calculator helped ground those discussions in something every organization cares about: business impact. It gave attendees a way to estimate how document automation could affect their own operations and helped move the conversation from technical possibilities to practical outcomes.

I think we were really taken aback by the diversity of perspectives in the room. People came from different industries and business environments, and brought very different operational realities and use cases to the discussion.

We were surprised that many of the issues being discussed were incredibly similar. Within all industries, companies had a lot of the same challenges around managing documents, approvals, integrations and keeping processes consistent. 

While working on practical document-processing scenarios, it became evident that the challenges most companies face go far beyond document extraction. As operations expand across multiple departments and teams, many organizations are still faced with fragmented workflows, repetitive manual validations, inconsistent document formats and processes that are becoming harder to manage.

And the workshop also reinforced something we often see when working with SAP technologies: real conversations happen once people can interact directly with a solution. The hackathon became much more practical and grounded after participants started to set up schemas, test classifiers and experiment with workflows. Rather than considering AI as something far away or theoretical, attendees began to consider how these solutions could realistically be integrated into their own operational environments.

Another important conclusion we arrived at was the importance of integration for successful AI adoption. Precise extraction models are crucial, no one will deny that, but they are just one part of the process. Companies also require workflows capable of adapting to operational changes, handling variations between document types, and collaborating effectively over time with existing systems.

Some Reflections

One of the most gratifying aspects of the workshop was to look back and see how quickly participants started to relate the exercises to their own organizations. Throughout the day the conversation evolved easily from examples under discussion to real business processes, internal priorities and potential next steps. Watching that transition in real time was a reminder of how the value of these sessions often goes far beyond the tech itself.

The workshop was also a great opportunity for us to hear directly from organizations that are facing different operational challenges, and to get a better understanding of how they are approaching document automation and AI adoption within their SAP landscapes.

Some of the most valuable insights came not only from the practical tasks but from the conversations that took place around them. The people who came had different perspectives, experiences and business contexts and we learnt from each other. The workshop was a valuable experience for all involved. As for me, I’m already looking forward to the next one, whether that’s as a host, a participant, or simply someone eager to keep learning.
